trajectory_summary: The call opened with a qualified, cooperative homeowner who had a clear bill pain signal, a fresh roof, and active comparison shopping — a strong lead. Parker navigated the tree constraint well and kept Warner engaged through the verify section. The call lost shape in the final two minutes: the bill collection hit a dead end (landline, no app access, bill discarded), the wife's scheduling role was never resolved into a concrete inclusion plan, and the close landed as an unanchored callback rather than a locked appointment. The lead is warm but the outcome is fragile.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ended with a soft callback promise — no appointment booked, no date locked, no bill collected. This should be tagged as 'callback_scheduled_unanchored' rather than any form of booked appointment. The callback has meaningful no-contact risk: Warner is heading to a doctor's appointment, is on a landline, and named his wife's MS schedule as the gating factor. Parker has no locked time and no confirmed channel for the follow-up.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Anchoring the callback — converting a soft deferral into a locked commitment
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Could you give me a callback in about an hour, hour and a half? And I'll have that information for you because I have to check my wife's calendar schedule.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Parker accepted an unanchored callback on a warm lead with a caregiver constraint, no bill, and no wife-availability confirmation. The call's entire value is now at risk of evaporating between now and the follow-up. The manager should walk Parker through the minimum anchoring moves: lock a specific time, confirm the channel, and ask whether the wife can be on the follow-up call. These are 30-second moves that convert a hope into a recoverable lead.

key_moments.quote: I was just looking at it. I'm looking at that, and I'm looking at generic too. I haven't decided which one I wanna go with.
key_moments.stewart_read: Warner names a competitor ('generic') and signals active comparison shopping in his first substantive answer. The already_saw_design rebuttal strategy was available here — 'it's always best to get a couple looks so you have something to compare against' — and Parker did not deploy it. Instead Parker moved to a generic reassurance ('we can definitely help you and see what it looks like for you'). The strategic read would be that Parker was holding the comparison framing for later; the reactive read is that he missed the opening to differentiate Ion early. Given that Parker never returned to the competitive framing, the reactive read holds.

key_moments.quote: Would you trim any of the branches back?
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker's branch-trimming pivot is a positive improvisation. Warner drew a hard line on tree removal; Parker found a middle path (trimming vs. removal) that kept Warner in the conversation. Warner accepted immediately. This is constraint-respecting qualification work — Parker assessed the satellite view and offered a feasible alternative rather than either capitulating or pushing back. The schema does not explicitly cover this move, but it aligns with the verify section's spirit of qualification-as-conversation.

key_moments.quote: It was a a few hours more than I had last year this time, so I just kinda tossed it.
key_moments.stewart_read: Warner reveals that his usage is rising year-over-year — he discarded the bill specifically because the number upset him. This is a second, stronger emotional signal of bill pain beyond the $331 figure already captured. Parker's response was to ask whether Warner could log into ComEd online — a procedural pivot that treated the disclosure as a data-collection problem rather than an emotional anchor. The flip was available: the rising usage is exactly the kind of pain that makes solar's value concrete. Parker captured the bill as data and dropped the emotional signal entirely.

key_moments.quote: So, yeah, just with it really does help us to get that. Do you know, like, would you be able to log in to your ComEd account online or, like, an app and kinda get a look at that for me real quick?
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker directs Warner to log into ComEd online without first establishing what device Warner is on or whether he has app access. This is where the tech-friction path was initiated incorrectly — not at 05:21 when the landline is revealed. Warner had already said he gets the bill by mail and discarded it; the online pivot was a reasonable next step, but asking without a device check set up a dead end. When Warner revealed he was on a landline heading to a doctor's appointment, the path collapsed entirely. The landline_protocol pivot (verbal readback or defer to specialist) was available and unused.
